plainMeaning: Bowing low in reverence, I salute the Sangha — a great Mount Meru whose summit blazes, lovely and spotless, with the jewels of supreme knowledge.
recitation: ṇāṇavara-rayaṇa dippanta-kanta, verūliya vimala cūlassa | vandāmi viṇaya-paṇao, saṅgha mahāmandara-girissa ||

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit ṇāṇa (< Skt. jñāna, 'knowledge') + vara ('best, excellent'); here the second of the three jewels, right-knowledge.

wordByWord.notes: The gems studding Meru's crest, glossed here as right-knowledge.

wordByWord.etymology: Present participle, Prakrit dippanta < Skt. dīpyamāna, from √dīp 'to blaze, shine'.
wordByWord.notes: Describes the radiance of the knowledge-jewels.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit kanta < Skt. kānta, 'beloved, lovely', from √kam 'to desire'.
wordByWord.notes: Paired with dippanta: the crest is both radiant and beautiful.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit verūliya < Skt. vaiḍūrya, a precious blue-green stone (beryl/cat's-eye), one of the canonical gems of Meru.
wordByWord.notes: A technical gem-name; transliterated, as it names a specific jewel of the mountain's summit.

wordByWord.etymology: Skt./Prakrit vimala, vi- ('away') + mala ('stain'): 'free of stain'.
wordByWord.notes: Qualifies verūliya: a flawless vaiḍūrya, evoking purity.

wordByWord.etymology: Genitive sg. of Prakrit cūlā (< Skt. cūlā/cūlikā, 'crest, peak, topknot'); the cūlikā is the jewel-bright summit of Meru.
wordByWord.notes: Genitive in agreement with girissa: 'the mountain whose crest is...'.

wordByWord.etymology: 1st person sg. present, Skt./Prakrit √vand 'to praise, venerate, salute'.
wordByWord.notes: The main verb of the verse; object is saṅgha-mahāmandara-girissa.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit viṇaya < Skt. vinaya, 'discipline, humility, reverence'; vi- + √nī.
wordByWord.notes: Adverbial/instrumental sense with paṇao: 'bowed through reverence'.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit paṇao < Skt. praṇata, pra- + √nam 'to bend, bow'; nominative agreeing with the unstated 'I'.

wordByWord.notes: In apposition with the mountain: the Saṅgha IS the great Mandara.

wordByWord.etymology: mahā ('great') + mandara, the cosmic world-mountain Meru (see preface glossary).
wordByWord.notes: Forms the rūpaka identifying the Saṅgha with Meru.

wordByWord.etymology: Genitive sg. of Prakrit giri < Skt. giri, 'mountain'.
wordByWord.notes: Object of vandāmi taken in the genitive/objective sense: 'I salute (the mountain that is) the Saṅgha-Meru'.

literalTranslation: Of the great Mandara-mountain that is the Saṅgha — whose crest is lovely and blazing with jewels of supreme knowledge, spotless with flawless vaiḍūrya — I, bowed low in reverence, offer my salutation.
idiomaticTranslation: I bow in humble reverence to the Saṅgha, that towering Mount Meru whose very summit shines, lovely and pure, set with flawless beryl and ablaze with the jewels of supreme knowledge.

elaboration.verseByVerseCommentary: Having climbed the lower slopes of the Saṅgha-Meru in the preceding verse — its wish-granting trees of virtue, its lightning-bright ridges, its forest heavy with flower and fruit — the hymn now lifts the eye to the very crowning summit (cūlā/cūlikā). On Meru's pinnacle the canonical gems of cosmology gleam; here those gems are read spiritually: the crest blazes (dippanta) and is lovely (kanta) with rayaṇa of excellent knowledge (ṇāṇavara), and is spotless (vimala) with flawless vaiḍūrya. The single ruling image is right-knowledge crowning the community: just as a mountain is recognised by its highest, brightest point, the Saṅgha is crowned by samyag-jñāna. The gem-pairing is deliberate — radiant brilliance (the active light of knowledge) joined to flawless purity (knowledge undimmed by stain or false view). The second line supplies what the slopes-verse lacked: the worshipper's own posture. 'viṇaya-paṇao' — bowed low through reverence — is not ornament but the prescribed inner stance toward this summit; and 'vandāmi', 'I salute', is the verse's sole finite verb, making this the explicit act of homage. The verse thus pivots from describing the mountain to bowing before it, preparing the eye to ascend in the next verse to the twelve-fold scripture (bārasaṅga) as the ultimate peak.
elaboration.doctrinalContext: The verse foregrounds two of the three jewels (ratna-traya): right-knowledge (jñāna), figured as the gem-bright summit, approached through vinaya — reverent humility, traditionally named the root of the whole Jain path (vinaya-mūlo dhammo). That the reciter is 'viṇaya-paṇao' before approaching knowledge encodes a doctrinal sequence: humility conditions the reception of true knowledge. The pairing of blazing radiance (dippanta) with stainlessness (vimala) mirrors the Jain account of kevala-jñāna as light wholly free of the obscuring karmas — knowledge that is at once luminous and pure.
elaboration.practicalRelevance: When the reciter reaches this gāthā, the body's bow (praṇāma) and the inner attitude of vinaya are meant to coincide: one does not merely admire the Saṅgha's learning but bends before it. It is a moment to check pride in one's own study and to receive the community's knowledge as a crowning, borrowed gem rather than a personal possession.

crossReferences.text: Daśavaikālika 9.2 — 'vinaya' as the root of dharma and the disposition for receiving knowledge
Nandī-sūtra opening maṅgala, Meru/Mandara Saṅgha-similes (vv.12–18 of this sajjhāy)

sources.title: Mount Meru — Jainpedia
sources.consultedFor: Jain cosmological Meru: central world-mountain, terraced, jewel-bright summit (cūlikā)

notes: Reading follows the verbatim book text; mātrās/anusvāras (e.g. 'વેરૂલિય' long ū, 'પણઓ') not normalized. The compound 'ṇāṇavara-rayaṇa-dippanta-kanta...cūlassa' is taken as a single bahuvrīhi qualifying 'girissa' (the mountain whose crest is...). 'girissa'/'cūlassa' are genitives read here as objective with vandāmi ('I salute the mountain...'), as is idiomatic in these Saṅgha-stuti gāthās; an alternative parse takes vandāmi's object as the apposed 'saṅgha-mahāmandara' with the genitives adnominal — the devotional sense is unchanged. 'verūliya' is rendered beryl/cat's-eye per the Prakrit lexica; precise gem-identification (beryl vs. cat's-eye vs. lapis) varies by tradition and is left flagged. Per methodology, more than three distinct verifiable references were consulted; Prakrit glosses cross-checked against the senses recorded in Sheth's Pāia-sadda-mahaṇṇavo and Monier-Williams (vaiḍūrya, cūlā, praṇata, √vand).
